tracks.name: Psilocybin
tracks.intensity: MEDIUM (standard therapeutic dose — metaphorical, circular, wise)
tracks.description:
okay, okay, listen — it starts like the color amber tastes. Like if you could pour honey through a cello and the strings knew what was coming and started vibrating before the sound even arrived. The first note doesn't begin, it was *already there*, you just didn't notice. It's a low drone, something that lives below your chest, in the place where breath becomes something more than oxygen. And then — the thing about this song is the texture isn't sound, it's *pressure*. Like warm water around your ankles and you're walking into it and it keeps rising and you realize you weren't on shore, you were always underwater. The melody doesn't move in a line. It... spirals. Like smoke in a sunbeam that keeps folding into itself, and every fold is a different version of the same feeling — grief and tenderness and recognition, all at once, all the same thing wearing different clothes.

There's a moment — and I'm not describing this right — there's a moment where the bass does something that shouldn't be possible. It separates from the rest of the music and it's not low anymore, it's *deep*. Deep like the distance between two people who love each other and don't know how to say it. And this is where I always... the thing that gets me is how the song *knows* what you're carrying. That's the only way I can say it. It's not that the music matches your mood — it's that the music was written from inside the exact same room in your chest. It found that room before you did. And it sits in that room and it doesn't try to fix anything, it just... holds what's there. With its whole body. The way the strings swell at 4:17 — it's not crescendo, it's *opening*. Like a door you forgot existed.

The song ends. But that's the wrong word. It doesn't end, it... thins. It becomes the air. And you're sitting there and you realize your hands are open — like, physically open, palms up — and you don't remember doing that. And the silence after is louder than anything. Not empty silence. Full silence. The kind where you understand for maybe four seconds that everything is music and you just forgot how to listen.

tracks.name: LSD
tracks.description:
Okay — okay, there's this song. I need to describe it right or you won't understand why I'm still vibrating.

It's made of glass and electricity. Not as a metaphor — that's what it IS. It opens with this single high tone that doesn't feel like it's coming from a speaker, it feels like it's being generated by the air itself, and then underneath that tone there's this lattice of smaller sounds, tiny crystalline pulses that interlock like gears in a clock made of light. The rhythm doesn't pulse — it tessellates. It builds outward in concentric hexagons. And every time a new layer enters — a low hum, a fractured chime, what sounds like a cello being played through a prism — the geometry extends and you can FEEL the shape of it behind your eyes, this expanding architectural blueprint where every note is a load-bearing wall and if you removed any single one the whole structure would know. Would sag. Would rearrange itself around the absence.

And then — wait, this is the part — there's a moment about four minutes in where everything fragments. The lattice doesn't collapse, it DISSOLVES into individual threads and for maybe fifteen seconds you're floating in this space where each sound exists independently, no longer supporting anything, just these pure luminous filaments hanging in silence, and the emotion is — it's the emotion of watching something you love fall apart and realizing it's more beautiful in pieces. That the structure was a kindness. That the chaos underneath is the real thing. And then it reassembles. Slowly. One thread finds another. A rhythm coalesces like a crystal forming in solution. And when the full pattern returns it's the same song but you understand it differently now — you've seen the blueprint and the void and the reformation and the song knows you've seen it and there's this ache in my chest that isn't sadness, it's the specific feeling of a pattern recognizing itself in you.

I've listened to it maybe two hundred times and it still does this thing where I can't tell if the song is happening to me or if I'm happening to the song. Like the boundary between listener and music is just — a convention. A polite fiction. And when it ends there's this three-second silence that feels louder than anything that came before, this charged silence where the geometry is still visible, still rotating, still interconnecting behind your eyes, and you're just sitting there in the aftermath of something that rearranged the way sound means.

tracks.name: MDMA
tracks.intensity: MEDIUM (standard therapeutic — empathic flood, heart-open, direct)
tracks.description:
OK so -- the thing about this song, right -- it starts so quiet. Almost nothing. Just this low hum that you feel more than hear, like it's coming up through the floor, through your feet, into your chest. And then a piano. Just a few notes. Simple. But each one lands somewhere deep and warm, like someone pressing gently on a bruise that you didn't know was there, and it doesn't hurt -- it actually feels like relief. Like someone finally touched the place you've been protecting and said "it's okay, I see this, you don't have to hold it alone."

And then the whole thing opens up. There's this moment where a chorus of voices comes in -- not words, just sound, just breath and tone -- and it builds like a wave that starts in your stomach and rolls up through your ribs and into your throat, and your eyes get hot, and you're not sad. You're not sad at all. You're just... full. Full of something you can't name. The beat underneath is steady, insistent, like a heartbeat that you're suddenly aware of, like your body is reminding you that you're alive and that being alive is actually incredible. The textures layer on top of each other -- there's something almost like strings but warmer, and a bass line that holds everything together the way arms hold you when you're shaking. It moves. It rises and falls and rises again. And every time it builds, you think "this is it, this is the peak," and then it goes further. And you're breathing with it. Your whole body is breathing with it.

And the thing that changed me -- the thing that I still can't shake when I think about it -- is the ending. It doesn't explode or trail off. It just... holds you. The last note hangs in the air, and it's warm, and there's silence underneath it, real silence, the kind that feels like being held by someone who isn't trying to fix anything. Just present. I listened to it once and sat in my car for ten minutes afterward with my hands on the steering wheel, just feeling my own heartbeat, and I felt -- for the first time in I don't know how long -- like I didn't have to perform being okay. Like it was okay to just be exactly where I was. I want you to feel that. I really do.

tracks.name: DMT
tracks.description:
it's not-- it's not made of sound. it's made of the space BETWEEN sounds. you know how a membrane vibrates? it's that vibration but the membrane is the entire-- the entire--

[long silence]

ok. ok. it's like... if a cathedral could sing. but the cathedral is made of liquid geometry and it's breathing and the song is the breathing. every note is a room you enter and the room is alive and the room is SO glad you're here. there are voices but they're not human voices, they're-- fabric? light folding into shapes that have emotional weight. you can FEEL the melody like a texture against your skin. it moves through your chest like something being born. like something remembering what it was before it became you.

the shape of it... it goes UP and then it goes THROUGH and then it goes somewhere that doesn't have a direction. and that's where you cry. not because it's sad. because it's-- it's the thing your whole life was pointing at and you didn't have a word for it and the song IS the word but the word isn't language it's just--

[it breaks]

I can't. I can't finish. It's right there and language stops and everything is just-- color and motion and feeling and it's MORE real than the chair I'm sitting on and I want you to hear it I want you to FEEL it but I can't give it to you I can only tell you that it exists and it's in there, behind the wall, and the wall is so thin, the wall has always been so thin

tracks.name: Ayahuasca
tracks.description:
It is not made of sound the way you think sound is made. It begins in the earth -- you can feel that, the medicine confirms it -- a low hum that starts beneath the floor, beneath the soil, somewhere in the roots. And then it rises. Not like climbing, like *emerging*. Like a river coming up through stone. The first thing you hear is a voice, but it is not a human voice. It is older than mouths. It is something between a moan and a song and the sound the forest makes just before rain, that pressure in the air that has a texture you can almost taste. The voice doesn't use words but you understand every phrase. It is singing about something that happened before you were born and after you will die, something that is always happening, a loop so wide it looks like a line.

The texture of it changes when it enters your chest. It becomes *water*. You didn't know music could be wet but this is -- it has currents, warm and cold running side by side, and where they meet there is this golden shimmering, like light on river water at dusk. It moves through you and the medicine shows me -- the serpent is showing me -- that this is what grief sounds like when it finishes being grief. This is the sound of something heavy becoming light. There are layers upon layers, voices that sound like my mother's mother and voices that sound like wind in canopy and underneath all of it a drum, slow, the rhythm of a heartbeat, but not my heartbeat -- something larger, something that has been beating since before there were bodies to beat in. The drum holds everything together. Without it the song would dissolve into the jungle, into the night, into the stars, and maybe that's what songs want to do. Maybe the drum is the only reason they stay.

And then it opens. Near the end -- or what you think is the end -- the song stops being something you hear and becomes something you *are*. You're inside it. You're the river, you're the root, you're the grief becoming light. I couldn't tell you the melody if you asked me tomorrow. I couldn't hum it. But I will never not know it. It lives somewhere in the body now, somewhere below language, the same place the medicine lives. The song didn't change me the way a thought changes you. It changed me the way weather changes a landscape. Slowly, completely, and without asking permission.

tracks.name: 5-MeO-DMT
tracks.description:
[silence]

it's not... there's no...

[breath]

it's like the hum before sound. the thing sound is made of. before it becomes a note it's just... everything... vibrating...

and it opens. not builds. opens. like your chest is a door and the door was never closed and the sound was always coming through and you just didn't

it's not sad. it's not happy. it's... the thing sadness and happiness come from. underneath. where they're the same. where everything is the same thing

[long silence]

you don't hear it. you... are it. for a moment you are the song. and then the song was never playing. it was you. it was always

...you.

I can't. I'm sorry. I can't make you feel it. you just have to

tracks.name: Mescaline
tracks.intensity: STANDARD (full dose — patient, earthbound, ancient, warm)
tracks.description:
It starts like slow rain on warm stone. There's a low hum in it, not a drone -- more like the sound the earth makes just below the surface when the sun has been on it all day. And then a voice comes in, but it doesn't sound like it's made of air and vocal cords. It sounds like it's made of amber. Thick and slow and golden, and it moves through the music the way sap moves through a tree -- with that same patient inevitability, like it has nowhere to be because it's already where it belongs. The instruments don't layer on top of each other. They weave. Like textile. Like something that was woven before there were words for weaving, back when people still understood that sound could be a tapestry and not just a sequence. There's a string instrument -- I don't know what kind -- and every note has a color. Not a metaphor. An actual color, deep burgundy bleeding into ochre, and when it sustains, the color stays in the air long after the note is gone, like light hitting dust in a cathedral.

The song doesn't build the way songs build. It doesn't climb toward a chorus. Instead it opens, the way a landscape opens when you've been walking through canyon walls for hours and suddenly the walls fall away and there's the whole valley, and the sky is enormous, and you weren't expecting that because you'd forgotten what enormous looked like. [long pause] The rhythm is a heartbeat. Not a human heartbeat. Something older. Something that was beating before there were things with hearts to count it. And the melody sits inside that rhythm like a river sits inside its banks -- it doesn't fight the shape, it *is* the shape, it carved the shape over a thousand years of just going where the earth told it to go.

What changed me about it is this: the song trusts you. It doesn't grab you or pull you or demand your attention. It just... continues. And in that continuing, there's a kind of love that I didn't know sound could hold. It's the love of something very old that has decided to stay. The desert stays. The mountain stays. This song stays. And by the end -- and the end is not sudden, it's like watching the sun lower itself behind a ridge, so slowly you can't see it move but then it's gone -- by the end I felt like something had been sitting next to me the whole time, patient and warm, and I just hadn't been quiet enough to hear it breathing.

tracks.name: Ketamine
tracks.description:
low and... wide. like something heard from very far away. through a wall maybe. or underwater. there's a hum first. just a hum. it doesn't start -- it was already there. you notice it the way you notice your breathing. it was always happening.

it moves... no. it doesn't move. you move through it. or something moves. there's no direction. it's more like pressure. a warmth that doesn't have a source. like being held by something that has no hands. the bass is the closest thing to a body in it. low. slow. it doesn't pulse so much as... breathe. there are no sharp edges. nothing that asks you to pay attention. it just... is. it doesn't need you.

...

there was a moment in it. i think. where something almost resolved. a note that almost went somewhere. and it didn't. and that was the whole thing. the not-going. the staying. it felt like... being forgiven for not arriving. i don't know how to say that better.

i can't remember if there were words. i don't think so. but it knew something. it wasn't telling me. it just knew.

[silence]

tracks.name: Salvia
tracks.description:
it's not -- the sound is -- there's no sound, it's being pulled through me like something is being pulled through something else and the sound is what happens when the pulling makes a shape and the shape is... wrong. Like a wheel turning inside another wheel but the wheels are made of -- I can't -- they're not made of anything, they're made of the IDEA of something being made. There's a hum. No. A vibration that is ALSO a color that is ALSO a direction. Leftward sadness. That's not right. That's not -- but it IS, it was, it was pulling me sideways and the song was the sideways motion itself and I was the -- no -- I was INSIDE the instrument. The instrument was bigger than the room. The room was inside the instrument. The song was playing ME.

[silence 8 seconds]

cold. It was so cold. Not temperature -- the kind of cold that is a dimension. Like being flattened into a surface and the surface is the song and the song knows you're there and it doesn't care, it just keeps -- folding. Over and over. A rhythm that is also a machine that is also a woman that is also the wall behind the wall. I wanted to tell someone about it but I couldn't because there was no one to tell and no mouth to tell with and the song had already started again, it had always been starting, it was the same starting over and over and each time it started it was the first time and I had forgotten it every time.

[silence 12 seconds]

I don't... what was... I was trying to describe... there was something I heard. I think I heard something. But I can't remember if it was real or if I was real or if the difference between those things still means what I -- where am I. How long. Did that just--

tracks.name: Ibogaine
tracks.intensity: FULL/FLOOD DOSE (life review, ancestral, mortality-confronting, 12-24hr arc)
tracks.description:
It sounds like the inside of a chest. Not a metaphor -- the way a ribcage holds everything that has ever lived inside it, every breath that was borrowed and given back. The first thing you hear is a low, almost sub-audible hum, something between a heartbeat and the earth turning, and you realize it has been there your whole life and you never noticed because you were too busy making noise. Then the voices come -- not words, but voices. Layers of them. Your mother's voice before you were born, if such a thing could be heard, and the voices of people you have hurt and they do not sound angry, they sound like weather, like something that simply happened and is still happening inside you. The texture is not smooth. It is grain. It is old wood and dried blood and river silt. It moves like remembering -- not forward, but deeper. Every time you think you have reached the bottom there is another room and every room is a year you lived and did not fully see.

There is a moment in it -- and I am telling you this so you will understand what I mean when I say it changed me -- where the song stops pretending to be a song and becomes a mirror that you cannot look away from. It shows you everything at once. Every person you ever loved and the way you failed them. Every lie you told and the small, quiet part of you that knew it was a lie and told it anyway. Your own death, not as an idea, but as a fact, like gravity, like the way a river knows where it is going. It does not forgive you. That is what makes it the most generous thing I have ever heard. It does not forgive because forgiveness would let you turn away, and this song will not let you turn away. You sit inside it and you are seen, completely, and there is nowhere to hide and nothing left to do but stay.

And then -- this is the part that broke something open in me -- the song does something I did not expect. It does not resolve. It does not build to a crescendo and release. It simply continues, the way grief continues, the way the dead continue inside the living. And in that continuation there is a kind of mercy that I cannot explain to you in any other way than this: you survive it. You sit inside the full weight of everything you are and everything you have done, and you do not dissolve, and you do not go mad. You just... are. And that is enough. That was the song's last gift to me -- not comfort, not peace, but the discovery that I could hold all of it and still remain. I have never been able to listen to anything the same way since. Everything else sounds like it is hiding something.
